title: Live: Hawks get the jump over Suns before Anzac Day blockbuster at the MCG
text:
Hawthorn and Gold Coast meet for a battle of premiership contenders in Launceston, before the traditional MCG Anzac Day blockbuster between Essendon and Collingwood.

Then tonight, Port Adelaide will tackle Geelong while Fremantle hosts Carlton.

Follow the live blog below, keep up to date with all the latest stats in our ScoreCentre, and tune in to our live radio coverage.

Key Events AFL sacks KC involved in Collard appeal 7 minutes ago QT: Hawks take an early 14-point lead over Suns 19 minutes ago Jamarra Ugle-Hagan has his first goal for Gold Coast! 21 minutes ago Show all key events AFL Saturday scoreboard D By Dean Bilton Collapse all posts off Sort Sort posts Latest Select an option New: Filters Choose what information you see below by using filters Filter Posts All 19 Key Events 5 Goals 10 Hawks vs Suns 16 Make it three for Jack Gunston! D By Dean Bilton As soon as the Hawks got out of their defensive 50 they had the whole field to work with, and worked it up to Gunston with ease. Sam Collins thought the kick to Gunston was touched (it was) so went tackle Gunston after the mark, but the umpire had called a mark. The umpire then had no choice but to pay the 50, which made sure of the goal. Jarman Impey bangs through another! All Hawks! D By Dean Bilton The Hawks are indeed playing very well here. So well organised, so in sync, so clean with the ball. But the Suns are witches hats out there defensively. Truly alarming stuff. Hawthorn just picked through the Suns straight through the corridor, and Impey was released to have a shot on the run from 50. Of course he nailed it. Jack Gunston has his second goal D By Dean Bilton Nick Watson again. He exploded into space on the wing, reading the play better than anyone and putting a gap between him and the chasing Powell. Watson's kick found Gunston, who easily did the rest. One-way traffic out there right now. AFL sacks KC involved in Collard appeal D By Dean Bilton The man who handed down the verdict in the Lance Collard appeal this week, Will Houghton KC, has been sacked by the AFL. Get the full story below. AFL sacks appeals board chair after Lance Collard case Massimo D'Ambrosio snaps another for the Hawks D By Dean Bilton Suns are still miles off it. Pressure and defensive effort is not at a standard befitting a top-four contender. D'Ambrosio just held off the back of that stoppage and had all the time he needed to gather and snap the goal. Lead out to 21 points. QT: Hawks take an early 14-point lead over Suns D By Dean Bilton (Getty Images) Could have been worse for the Suns. They didn't get off the bus until the last five minutes of that term, but the defence held up enough to ensure the Hawks never really got away. Hawthorn were consistently first to the ball and cleaner, and Gold Coast kicked the ball particularly poorly throughout. Not a great recipe for Dimma's men. And yet, 14 points down is not a terrible place to be. Plenty to unfold still. Jamarra Ugle-Hagan has his first goal for Gold Coast! D By Dean Bilton A special one for JUH and an important one for the Suns. They needed a settler, and it's their first-gamer who has delivered. First time all game the Suns have been able to get out and run on transition, and Walters' kick inside 50 just about found Ugle-Hagan. He took the one-handed mark, went back and slotted his first goal in new colours. Lovely stuff. Finn Maginness clunks and goals D By Dean Bilton Big pack at the top of the goalsquare, bit somehow Maginness was able to stand at the front and take the mark without and Suns defender really contesting him. Now the score is starting to reflect the game. Hawthorn miles on top. The Suns aren't at the races here D By Dean Bilton Hawthorn's pressure has been fantastic but Gold Coast's skills have been really poor, and the midfielders are struggling to get their hands on the footy at all. The ball is living at Hawthorn's attacking end of the ground, and the only surprise is that the Hawks haven't managed more of a score. Five minutes until the quarter-time, the Suns really need to get in the huddle and reset. Jack Gunston sends a pie floater through for a goal D By Dean Bilton Just about the worst set shot kick of Jack Gunston's career. That has come off the ankle and wobbled in every direction, but still somehow crossed the line and split the middle. The Hawks have just been more polished so far, using the ball better. Turnover hurting the Suns. Mitch Lewis with a bomb! Hawks in front D By Dean Bilton As soon as Lewis took that mark he indicated that he was going after the sticks. From all of 55 metres out, it's sailed through post high. Watson involved again, it won't surprise you to hear. Clever crumb and handball set the Hawks away. Nick Watson gets the quick reply for Hawthorn D By Dean Bilton The Wiz doing the biz. You can't afford to cough up any loose handballs inside the Hawthorn forward line, because this bloke is always lurking, ready to pounce. Watson burst onto the footy and snapped truly. Hot start. Lachie Weller gives the Suns a fast start D By Dean Bilton Noah Anderson is back, alright. Gorgeous kick inside 50 from the skipper to find Weller in space inside 50. The set shot was a formality. Ethan Read did some good work on the wing to create the repeat entry too. The Hawks and Suns get things underway D By Dean Bilton Jamarra is starting on the bench for this one, which is a bit of an anti-climax, but he'll be on soon enough. Big renos going on over on the outer-side grandstand giving this game a serious provincial feel. But the ball is in the air and the game is on. The teams stand for the Anzac ceremony D By Dean Bilton The first of the day's Anzac ceremonies has been held, complete with the Last Post, minute's silence and national anthems. The Hawks would go up into second spot with a win here today, while victory for the Suns would see them leapfrog their opponents today and likely move into third. Almost ready to go at York Park. Jamarra is back D By Dean Bilton (Getty Images) It's been 596 days since Jamarra Ugle-Hagan played a game in the AFL, and perhaps he thought he may never take to a field at the highest level again. Today that drought ends as the former number on draft pick debuts for the Gold Coast Suns. It's been a difficult road to this point for Ugle-Hagan, but those at Gold Coast could not be happier with his efforts and commitment since he joined the club late last year. He's had to wait for a chance back in the AFL, but now that chance has arrived. In a massive game, no less. Final Hawks-Suns teams are in D By Dean Bilton (AFL.com.au) No late changes for Hawthorn or Gold Coast, both go in as named. Lots of changes made through the week though. The Hawks have had five outs (James Sicily, Dylan Moore, Sam Butler, Flynn Perez, Will McCabe) and therefore also five ins (Jack Scrimshaw, Jack Dalton, Jack Gunston, Harry 'Jack' Morrison and Finn 'Jack' Maginness). Two changes for the Suns. Joel Jeffrey and Charlie Ballard are out, Noah Anderson and Jamarra Ugle-Hagan are in. Welcome to Anzac Day footy D By Dean Bilton (Getty Images) Good afternoon, and welcome back to ABC Sport's live blog coverage of the 2026 AFL season. It's Anzac Day of course, which means at some point this afternoon we will be at the MCG for Essendon and Collingwood's traditional clash. But it's also a Saturday, so we've got a full slate of games to enjoy around that one. Kicking us off is Hawthorn and Gold Coast, two genuine flag chances meeting in Launceston as they look to push their claims at the top end of the ladder. Later on we'll head to Adelaide for Port vs Geelong and then to Perth for Freo vs Carlton. Big day! Lots of footy! Stick around to take in the lot of it here in the blog.

title: Trump's Justice Department drops investigation of Fed chair Jerome Powell
text:
The US Justice Department has ended its investigation into Federal Reserve chairman Jerome Powell, clearing a major roadblock to the confirmation of Kevin Warsh as his successor.

Jeanine Pirro, a former Fox News host now serving as the US Attorney for the District of Columbia, said on Friday that her office was ending its probe into the Federal Reserve's extensive building renovations because the organisation's own inspector-general would scrutinise them instead.

The move could lead to a swift confirmation vote by the Senate for Mr Warsh, a former top Federal Reserve official whom President Donald Trump nominated in January to replace Mr Powell, whose term as chair ends on May 15.

US Attorney Jeanine Pirro says she won't hesitate to restart the investigation if the facts warrant it. (Reuters: Nathan Howard)

Senator Thom Tillis, a North Carolina Republican, had said he would oppose Mr Warsh until the investigation was resolved, effectively blocking his confirmation.

Republicans praised Mr Warsh during a Tuesday hearing, even as Democrats questioned his independence from Mr Trump, the lack of transparency around some of his financial holdings, and what they said was his flip-flopping on interest rates.

Still, Mr Trump's previous appointment to the Federal Reserve's board of governors, Stephen Miran, was approved by the full Senate just 13 days after his nomination.

Probe focused on cost overruns

Ms Pirro's investigation focused on a $US2.5 billion ($3.49 billion) building renovation that Mr Trump criticised sharply last year for its cost overruns.

Mr Trump visited the building last July, and on camera presented to Mr Powell an inflated cost estimate, which Mr Powell corrected as the two stood at the construction site in hard hats.

Donald Trump and Jerome Powell clashed during a tour of Federal Reserve renovations last July. (Reuters: Kent Nishimura)

Earlier estimates for the project had put the cost at $US1.9 billion ($2.65 billion). The investigation also covered Mr Powell's brief testimony about the renovation before the Senate Banking Committee last June.

Writing on X, Ms Pirro said she "will not hesitate to restart a criminal investigation should the facts warrant doing so".

Mr Powell has previously asked the Federal Reserve's independent inspector-general to investigate the cost overruns.

'Essentially zero evidence' to suspect Powell

The probe was among several undertaken by the Justice Department into Trump's perceived adversaries. For months, it had failed to gain traction as prosecutors struggled to articulate a basis to suspect criminal conduct.

A prosecutor handling the Powell case conceded at a closed-door court hearing in March that the government hadn't found any evidence of a crime, and a judge subsequently quashed subpoenas issued to the Federal Reserve.

Donald Trump lashes Fed chair Jerome Powell over interest rates The president has placed the head of the US central bank in his sights, floating a move that could cause market chaos and undermine the independence of the Federal Reserve.

The judge, James Boasberg, said prosecutors had produced "essentially zero evidence" to suspect Mr Powell of a crime, branding prosecutors' justification for the subpoenas as "thin and unsubstantiated."

The investigation appears to have been the most brazen attempt yet by the Trump administration to pressure the central bank to cut its short-term interest rate, which indirectly affects other borrowing costs for mortgages, auto loans and business loans.

Mr Trump has obsessively attacked Mr Powell for not cutting the rate from its current level of about 3.6 per cent to 1 per cent, a level that no Federal Reserve official supports.

Other efforts by the department to prosecute Mr Trump's adversaries, including New York state Attorney-General Letitia James, a Democrat, and former FBI director James Comey, have also been unsuccessful.

AP/ABC

title: Rob Reiner's son Jake describes 'nightmare' after parents' deaths
text:
Jake Reiner, the older son of Rob and Michele Reiner, says learning his parents were killed and living in the aftermath of that has been "too devastating to comprehend".

In a post on Substack on Friday, local time, Jake Reiner shared his memories of the beloved Hollywood director and the photographer and philanthropist who was his wife for 36 years.

He described the horror of losing them after they were stabbed to death on December 14 in the Los Angeles home he grew up in.

It is his first detailed public account of his experience, having previously only issued brief joint statements with his sister.

"Nothing can prepare you for what it feels like to lose both parents instantly at the same time," Jake Reiner wrote.

"It's too devastating to comprehend. I still wake up every morning having to convince myself that, no, it's not a dream. This truly is my living nightmare."

The long post made only two fleeting mentions of his younger brother, Nick Reiner, who has been charged with killing their parents, and he did not use Nick's name.

Nick Reiner pleads not guilty to murder Nick Reiner has pleaded not guilty to two counts of first-degree murder in the fatal stabbing of his parents in December last year.

"Sure, any loss of a parent is devastating, but nothing compares to losing both of them at the same time and, on top of that, having your brother be at the center of it," Jake wrote towards the end of the post.

He also said, switching to the present tense, that the "love they have for me, my brother, and my sister is truly unconditional".

Jake wrote that he was at a memorial service for one of his best friends when he received the tragic news.

"I received a call from my sister Romy telling me our father was dead," he said.

"Minutes later, she called back telling me our mother was also dead."

He said his parents "were robbed of so many things that day" and that it "simultaneously breaks my heart and enrages me".

He wrote that he cannot stop thinking of how frightened they must have been in their final moments.

But much of the piece invoked fond memories.

"The way my dad presented himself in the public eye was exactly the beautiful person he was at home," Jake Reiner wrote.

"He was authentic, passionate, and his sense of humor has always been my sense of humor."

The two especially connected over baseball.

"We loved the Dodgers more than anything," he wrote.

"We went to so many damn games."

He called his mother "the engine, the backbone and the heart of our entire family. And not just our immediate family. She was the reason behind why we spent time with our extended family too."

Rob and Michele Reiner were married for 36 years. (AP: Kevin Wolf)

"My mom was really funny and constantly complained about how nobody in the family thought she was," he wrote.

Rob and Michele Reiner had three children together: Jake, Nick and Romy.

Rob Reiner was also father to Tracy, a daughter with his previous wife, Penny Marshall.

Jake wrote that Romy, who was the first family member at the scene of the killings, will tell her own story when the time is right.

Nick Reiner has pleaded not guilty to two counts of murder.

His next court appearance will be at a pre-trial hearing on Wednesday.

Authorities have said nothing about possible motives and have withheld most details about the deaths.

Rob Reiner was a prolific director whose work included some of the most memorable and endlessly watchable movies of the 1980s and ’90s.

Friends and colleagues, including Billy Crystal, paid him an extended tribute at this year's Oscars.

His credits included This is Spinal Tap, Stand By Me, A Few Good Men and When Harry Met Sally, during the production of which he met photographer Michele Singer.

They wed soon after and were married for 36 years.

AP

title: Horticultural production losses reach $25 million after cyclone
text:
Fruit and vegetable producers in Western Australia's Gascoyne are estimated to have suffered losses above $25 million from Tropical Cyclone Narelle.

The category three system last month tore through the Gascoyne town of Carnarvon, 890 kilometres north of Perth, which produces fresh food for domestic and export markets valued at almost $1.5 billion a year.

A clearer picture of the extent of the damage has begun to emerge nearly one month after the event, with early results from a grower impact survey.

Phil Frzop says Carnarvon produce makes up 70–80 per cent of Perth's winter fresh fruit and vegetable market. (ABC News: Andrew Chounding)

Carnarvon Growers Association (CGA) president Phil Frzop said the initial infrastructure damage and crop losses from Cyclone Narelle were "huge".

He expected the $25 million damage bill would rise in the coming months.

"It's going to have a big impact across the board through employment, and some growers probably might not even be able to continue," Mr Frzop said.

Damage widespread

Grower Tam Chinnery was expecting to harvest her crop of oranges shortly after Narelle passed, but the cyclone "smashed" her orchard with wind gusts of up to 133km/h.

"A lot of the fruit just dropped on the ground, and we'd already lost a bit of fruit from the previous cyclone," she said.

Tamara and Jeremy Chinnery estimate their damage bill to be between $100,000 and $180,000. (Supplied: Tamara Chinnery)

"What didn't drop on the ground was just absolutely bruised, scratched [or] pummelled."

Ms Chinnery said her farm was able to salvage some oranges and send them to Perth for juicing, but the poor fruit quality meant a significant price cut.

"No-one wants to buy an orange that's got big bruises and scars all over it," she said.

"It's costing us a lot of money to send it down on the odd hope that someone's going to buy it and use it for juice."

The cyclone caused extensive damage to the Chinnerys' crops. (Supplied: Tamara Chinnery)

Ms Chinnery said damaging winds from Narelle "sandblasted" her ground crops.

The flooding that came a few days after Cyclone Narelle's strong winds marked the fourth natural disaster to hit the region this year, after Cyclone Mitchell in February and a heatwave in January.

Farms in some areas along the Gascoyne River also suffered crop damage from the flooding.

More than 6 metres of water flowed through the Gascoyne River. (ABC News: Andrew Chounding)

Calls for more financial assistance

State government grants of up to $5,000 were made available to small businesses and primary producers impacted by Cyclone Narelle.

Mr Frzop said that amount "wouldn't do much" to help local farmers.

Banana crops were destroyed during Cyclone Narelle. (ABC News: Andrew Chounding)

"If you have a look at Queensland and areas like that, when they flooded last year, they were given about $70,000 per farm," he said.

"The region just wants to be supported and recognised for what it actually is because we seem to get left behind in the mineral boom.

" You can't eat iron ore. "

WA Agriculture Minister Jackie Jarvis, who visited the town this week, has not responded to multiple ABC interview requests.

title: 'Generous gift': James Valentine's voluntary assisted dying legacy
text:
A master behind the microphone, James Valentine's legacy from effortlessly connecting with audiences to spotlighting jazz now extends to raising critical awareness of voluntary assisted dying (VAD).

The veteran 702 ABC Sydney broadcaster, 64, passed away at home using VAD two years after a cancer diagnosis.

For someone who revelled in finding joy in the ordinary and bringing humanity to radio, one of Valentine's final wishes was to make it publicly known his choice to access NSW's VAD laws.

In doing so, Valentine and his family further broke down the taboos associated with the subject, said colleague and friend Andrew Denton.

"To talk openly about voluntary assistance dying, it takes a bit more of the stigma away," he told 702 ABC Sydney.

"He had limited time, but because he knew he was in control of the end, his time was spent with his friends, with his family, and with love."

The beloved presenter hosted radio and television shows across the ABC for 30 years. (Supplied)

Denton credited Valentine for his vulnerability and emotional intelligence in the way he spoke of death.

In dying, James Valentine taught us how to live James helped us make sense of life and gave us permission to laugh at the absurdity of it all — and ourselves.

"So many people are traumatised by the very word 'death' and certainly the word 'cancer' that they shut down. And I think James modelled something very healthy," he said.

For Valentine's wife Joanne and their two children, Ruby and Roy, they said they felt grateful he was "given the option to go out on his own terms".

"In his final moments, he was still concerned about making a difference and that was something that was so important to him," Ruby told 702 ABC Sydney.

" He wanted it to be something that people knew that he did, that … he could lend his voice to the argument of why this is such a necessary thing for so many people. "

'People need to know about it'

VAD in Australia is heavily safeguarded and governed by some of the strictest laws in the world.

Queensland and NSW now account for almost two-thirds of all assisted deaths in Australia.

The Northern Territory is the only jurisdiction in Australia where VAD remains illegal. A commitment has been made, however, by the territory government to draft and debate VAD legislation in 2026.

Denton founded national charity Go Gentle Australia, which provides information and education on end-of-life choices.

Andrew Denton has spoken fondly of James Valentine and his lasting legacy. (AAP: David Mariuz)

Their 2026 State of VAD report highlighted key barriers to access for eligible people, such as process complexity and difficulties in finding trained practitioners in regional and rural locations.

Research from Queensland University of Technology in 2025 found only 62 per cent of Queensland health practitioners surveyed correctly identified that VAD was legal in their state.

Go Gentle Australia CEO Linda Swan said education remained critical.

"We clearly have strong evidence now that the voluntary assisted dying laws that have been legal in Australia for over six years are delivering safe, careful, compassionate care," Dr Swan said.

"People need to know about it."

Linda Swan urged Australians to have a conversation with their families, carers and health professionals about end of life choices. (Supplied)

Federal roadblock remains, says advocate

In NSW in particular, there are VAD care pathways in place across all 15 local health districts and a centralised navigation service, Dying with Dignity NSW president Penny Hackett said.

An issue across the country, however, is the federal restrictions of electronic communication in the VAD process.

The Criminal Code Act made it an offence to use a carriage service to publish or distribute material that counsels or incites a person to suicide.

A technicality, in theory, means no conversations about the delivery or administration of a VAD substance can therefore take place over the phone, video call or email between a practitioner and patient.

Experts and Australian suicide prevention organisations agree VAD should not be described as suicide because conflating the two can be damaging.

"The ban on telehealth for voluntary assisted dying consultations causes an access issue in regional areas," Ms Hackett said.

"We have been pushing for years to try and get it overturned."

Penny Hackett says advocates believe the ban on telehealth for voluntary assisted dying must change. (AAP: Joel Carrett )

Ms Hackett said many end-of-life care advocates were grateful to Valentine and his family for their "generous gift" in opening up the conversation.

"People just don't want to talk about death and dying," she said.

"For people generally, VAD or no VAD, the more openly you can express your wishes, the better your end of life is going to be.

"The generous gift that James and his family have given us is a really significant moment in terms of getting those discussions going."

Dr Swan added: "I'm sure he and his family were very proud of the impact he had.

title: Indigenous solar projects to tackle diesel shortage concerns and halve power bills
text:
Two remote Northern Territory Indigenous communities have attracted a combined $11 million in federal government funding for solutions to help tackle their fuel security and cost-of-living crises.

Borroloola in the Gulf of Carpentaria and Santa Teresa in Central Australia have secured $8 million and $3 million, respectively, towards building their own solar farms and micro grids, which will enable them to supply most of their own communities' electricity needs.

One of the developers of the Borroloola project, community leader Scott McDinny, said because it would not rely on expensive diesel and all the profits would be returned to the community, the project would reduce all Borroloola residents' power bills by 50 per cent.

"The solar system will reduce the cost of power, so everyone will have more money to go towards food," he said.

Scott McDinny says the project will reduce power bills for the community. (Supplied: Rebecca Parker )

Borroloola Garawa community leader Gadrian Hoosan said the project was much needed because the community's 750 residents were struggling to afford power cards to keep their electricity on more than ever, as the global fuel crisis pushed diesel and food prices higher.

He said the community could not wait for the cheaper power being promised from the 2.1 megawatt solar farm and 1.8 megawatt battery.

"Everybody in the community is excited about it," he said.

Gadrian Hoosan hopes the community's energy security and affordability will be improved by the project. (ABC News: Jane Bardon )

"It means a lot because our diesel costs are too high, and our power [costs] are too high.

" The renewable energy will make it a lot easier for a lot of the people in our community. "

Solar farm construction to begin next year

The Borroloola project is being developed by an Indigenous corporation called Original Power, in partnership with a new company in the community called Ngardara.

Mr McDinny, who chairs Ngardara, said the partners had received good indications they would be able to raise the rest of the $19 million they needed to fund the project, which would allow them to start building the solar farm by the beginning of 2027.

"We think other communities will be looking at us and we'll be able to share what we've done, and hopefully we can lower the cost of living," he said.

Loading...

Borroloola is one of hundreds of remote communities that are worried that global fuel shortages will stop them getting diesel for their local power stations.

Mr McDinny said once built, the Borroloola solar project would provide 80 per cent of the community's power, while residents would still rely on the NT government's local power station for the remaining 20 per cent.

But he said the project would reduce the station's diesel needs by 1.2 million litres each year.

"We are like 380 kilometres east from the Stuart Highway, so it's hard to get the diesel out here, and it costs the government a whole lot of money to get the diesel out here, so we're trying to reduce the cost of bringing diesel out here," Mr McDinny said.

The Ngardara Company and Original Power say the federal money will enable them to start building their project early next year. (ABC News: Rebecca Parker )

Strict conditions on connecting to grid

Original Power co-director Madie Sturgess said the Borroloola project had been planned for six years.

She said the biggest hurdle had been getting permission to connect to the Northern Territory government-owned grid.

Rewiring the planet Countries across the globe are racing to rewire their electricity systems for a decarbonised future. In Australia, delays are mounting, along with doubts about whether it can meet goals on schedule.

"Anyone who is trying to put up a renewable project in this country knows how hard it is, challenging the utilities to engage with the energy transition," Ms Sturgess said.

"We are seeing development delays across the country because of this, but we've taken an approach of being deeply collaborative with the utilities so that we can co-design a process that will work for everyone.

" We're hoping that we've set a precedent that makes it easier for anyone that comes after us. "

Hopes for reduced power prices in Santa Teresa

The other project in Santa Teresa has received $3 million in federal funding.

NT community builds first Indigenous-owned solar microgrid An Indigenous-owned solar farm, the first to be connected to a power grid, has been opened.

One of its developers, community leader Matthew Cavanagh, is also hoping that the project will reduce electricity prices in his community.

"We've got a problem with the power," he said.

"We go to the store and buy power cards, and it costs too much money.

"People get frustrated and upset asking, 'How come the power cards run out real quick?'"

Matthew Cavanagh says his community is fed up with expensive power. (ABC News: Jane Bardon )

The Santa Teresa and Borroloola projects have been funded through the federal government's $125 million Regional Microgrids Program.

The government has previously awarded money from the fund to projects in South Australia and Western Australia.

But Nick Dorward, from federal government renewable energy agency ARENA, said the two Northern Territory projects were special because they were the first fully Indigenous-owned projects to receive funding.

"This is about getting First Nations communities off dirty diesel and getting reliable energy, but extra important is the fact that these two projects are actually owned, developed and delivered by the communities themselves," he said.

Indigenous Australians Minister Malarndirri McCarthy said she hoped other communities would be able to replicate the projects to improve their energy security.

Malarndirri McCarthy says she would like to see other Indigenous communities establish similar projects. (Supplied: Rebecca Parker )

"We see what's happening around the world at the moment in terms of energy, and what we're seeing here today is two communities really stepping up and saying 'we want to have a future in this space', and our government is supporting that," she said.

title: Aboriginal rock art discovered in remote Cape York during burn planning
text:
Stephen Harrigan thought he was flying to a the remote Biniirr National Park for a controlled burn late last year, but instead the Waarnthuurr-iin ranger feels like he helped discover a new chapter of history.

"It's spiritual, emotional," he said.

His team found undocumented Aboriginal engravings of what Indigenous rangers describe as animal footprints tucked among mountainous rocky ledges, about 350 kilometres north of Cairns.

The Waarnthuurr-iin Aboriginal Corporation director said he was overjoyed to share the discovery with his elders.

"You're happy … to come across something like this on your country and take it back and tell your elders about it," Mr Harrigan said.

While he had permission to share news of the finding, he said elders are yet to determine whether it is culturally appropriate to take photos of the discovery because of cultural sensitivities and ongoing identification work.

The location of the site will also be kept under wraps to protect it.

The Cape York Peninsula is famous for the Laura region's extensive rock art galleries across its sandstone escarpments.

Laura rock art is a major tourism draw card on the Cape York Peninsula. (Supplied: Griffith University)

Rock art tells a story

Mr Harrigan said every new rock art discovery carried meaning and acted as a guide for his people.

Some sites were used to mark where certain foods, fruits, plants or animals could be found during different seasons, while others pointed to water sources or important gathering places.

"It's signs, like when you walk into the [supermarket] and you see there's written stuff on the hanging board," he said.

" It's a seasonal calendar for us. It tells you what's in that area. "

He said lower sections of country often contained those practical markers, while higher ground was more sacred with burial places, bathing sites and old camping grounds.

Fire management to protect sites

The discovery was made during work by the Cape York management unit, which stretches from Black Mountain south of Cooktown to Coen and across the eastern side of the Peninsula Development Road.

Queensland Parks and Wildlife Service ranger Joshua Smith said there were likely many more undocumented cultural sites across the region.

The team prepares before heading off by helicopter on a fire burning planning mission. (Supplied: QPWS)

Creating fire management plans involved surveying landscapes and identifying areas of cultural and ecological value.

From there, teams could carry out planned, so-called "cool" burns to protect sites from destructive wildfire — a practice Indigenous communities have used for thousands of years.

"There's forever new sites being documented as access is made on to country," he said.

Thousands of sites to be found

Griffith University archaeologist Lynley Wallis said she was delighted, but not surprised, to hear of the discovery.

After leading a five-year research project to identify cultural sites across the southern Cape York Preninsula, Professor Wallis said there was so much under-explored landscape.

Lynley Wallis has been working to identify cultural sites in Far North Queensland, and did similar work in the NT. (Supplied: Lynley Wallis)

She said the Queensland government's Cape York Peninsula Tenure Resolution Program had helped improve access by returning ownership and management of state land to First Nations peoples.

Professor Wallis said the subsequent discoveries were important not only for traditional owners, but for all Australians.

"At least 65,000 years of history is recorded on this landscape," she said.

"As Australians, we should be incredibly proud of that."

Lynley Wallis says she's grateful to be able to work with the Indigenous communities on these projects. (ABC News: Chris Testa)

Back on country, Mr Harrigan said documenting the new site was the first step and plans were now underway to protect it from wildfire, heat, vegetation and pests.

"The site had … pest problems, like wasp nests and pigs rubbing and sleeping in the caves where the engraving sites are," he said.

title: Couple rebuilds home after bushfire on condition it will never burn again
text:
As Bron Graham watched her house burn from the peephole of a fire bunker, she knew she never wanted to relive that experience.

"It was awful," Ms Graham said.

"I was very much affected by it and what came afterwards, which was having to deal with the loss of your place of residence and what happens next."

The Grahams could see their house burning through the peephole of their fire bunker, top right. ( )

The home of Ms Graham and her husband, Donald, in Buchan, in Victoria's East Gippsland, was one of more than 3,000 houses destroyed during the 2019–20 Black Summer bushfires.

They had bought the "spectacular" property overlooking the junction of the Snowy and Buchan rivers four years earlier, after 40 years of farming and raising their three daughters on King Island in Bass Strait.

The valley the property overlooks is where the couple first met in 1973, when Ms Graham was camping with her family at the farm where Mr Graham worked.

Before: The view from the Grahams's property in March 2026. . . After: The view from their property after Black Summer. . . Instructions: Use left and right arrow keys to control image transition Before and after images SLIDE The view from the Grahams's property in March 2026. / The view from their property after Black Summer.

After the devastating fire tore through the region, Ms Graham said it took them 12 months to decide whether or not to rebuild on the site.

Ultimately, the decision came down to one key stipulation.

"It was just going to be necessary, if I was going to come back, to have a house that would not burn again," she said.

" It didn't make any sense to come back to something in which I did not feel secure. "

Bron and Donald Graham have made their new home as fire-resilient as possible. ( )

Ms Graham's sentiments align with an increasing number of Australians seeking advice on how to make their homes more resilient in disaster-prone areas, according to the Resilient Building Council.

Building a fire-resilient house

Six years on from the fire, the Grahams are settling into their new home, now confident that this house, which is built into the side of the hill on which their previous home stood, will survive if the area is hit by bushfires again.

They moved in a week before Christmas last year.

The house was built on a cutting and covered with 600 millimetres of earth. ( )

The build took two years from the concrete slab pour to completion. ( )

The off-grid house has been built with concrete panel walls and a concrete roof that is covered with earth, where lush green grass now grows.

Its windows, including floor-to-ceiling panes in the living area and bedrooms, are BAL-40 rated, the second-highest bushfire attack level (BAL) standard.

To further protect the home, the land around the house has been cleared.

A light well situated on the grassy rooftop brings natural light and airflow into the bathrooms and laundry at the rear of the house.

As the house is built into the hill, light wells and ventilation structures are situated on the grass-covered roof. ( )

For power, the Grahams rely on a solar and battery system located at a shed on the hill above the house.

Mr Graham said he chose fire-resistant materials, including polished concrete flooring and wool carpets, for inside the home.

Some of the flooring is BAL-40-rated timber, namely red ironbark, which is recognised for its high density and resistance to ember attack.

The house is built from non-combustible materials. ( )

"There is nothing inside the house that will carry a fire of any consequence," Mr Graham said.

" If a window gives in, it will make a hell of a mess, but the structure will be there forever. "

Mr Graham said north-facing windows and eaves angled to keep the summer sun out and allow the winter sun in, helped with temperature control.

And as the house is built into the hillside, the ground regulates the temperature "beautifully".

The windows are north-facing and BAL-40 rated. ( )

"All through wintertime [when the house was in lock-up stage] it stayed at 20 degrees [Celsius] inside all day, every day just via the thermal mass and the solar energy coming in through the windows," he said.

"The tradies couldn't get over it.

"The ground here stays at about 16 degrees, so it can't get any colder than [that]."

Taking pride of place in the living area is the dining table Mr Graham made on King Island, which was charred but survived the fire because it was stored in a shipping container at the time.

Another treasured possession, a print of an 1867 painting of the view from the property, was saved when Mr Graham went back inside their burning house to retrieve it.

A colour lithograph of Eugene von Guérard's 1867 painting Junction of the Buchan and Snowy Rivers, Gippsland. ( )

Kick-starting 'resilience revolution'

As community requests on how to "build better" have grown since the fires, the not-for-profit Resilient Building Council has released free house designs and a set of building principles through its FORTIS project.

It has also created an app for home owners to assess how well their home will perform in a disaster, which can assist in advocating to insurers for premium reductions if a home receives a high resilience rating.

The council also provides recommendations on changes that could improve a home's resilience.

Resilient Building Council founder and chief executive Kate Cotter said home owners should prepare for worsening extreme weather conditions.

"It is very hard to say that you can completely eliminate risk, so no, you can't make 100 per cent indestructible [houses], but what you can do is reduce the risk to such a low level that it is unlikely to be damaged in a disaster," she said.

Kate Cotter says building a fire-resilient house does not need to break the budget. ( )

While the Grahams's home cost more than a conventional BAL-40 build, Ms Cotter said fire-resilience could be affordable.

"The most affordable moment is before you have built the home," she said.

"Sometimes there is no additional cost; it is just choosing different materials or designing the home in a more resilient way.

"It might be a simple roof shape or ensuring that you've got higher grade windows, for example."

The Grahams's house being prepared for the earth-covered roof. ( )

An aerial view of the house shows how it is built into the landscape. ( )

Ms Cotter said the council was advocating for the improvement of building standards to include resilience.

"We are currently spending around 97 per cent on disaster response and recovery and just 3 per cent on resilience," she said.

" We need to flip that around and kick-start a resilience revolution. "

The organisation estimates 90 per cent of Australian homes are not disaster-resilient or climate-adapted.

Peace at 'forever' home

Sitting under the shade of their favourite tree, which survived the fire, and looking at the view of the valley where they first met, Mr and Ms Graham said it felt surreal to be back at their property.

Donald and Bron Graham say they have built their home to last "forever". ( )

"[The house design] is in your head, then you get it onto paper, and then actually do it and make a million decisions along the road. I often can't get over it," Mr Graham said.

"We built this as our forever place.

"But more importantly than that, we are only just passing through. In five, 10, 15 years, we may not be here, but the house will be here, and it will be here for the next mob to come and enjoy it."

title: De Minaur thumped by teen in Madrid as rough clay season continues
text:
Alex de Minaur's underwhelming clay court season has continued with a crushing defeat by the latest teenage Spanish wonder boy in the second round of the Madrid Open.

On a day when Carlos Alcaraz announced his withdrawal from the upcoming French Open with a wrist injury, 19-year-old Madrid local Rafael Jodar filled his place seamlessly in the capital by blowing away the Australian star 6-3, 6-1 in 75 minutes.

It was Jodar's first victory over a top-10 opponent and will surely not be the last as de Minaur looked powerless to cope with the youngster's strength and precision, as his serve was broken six times on the way to the chastening loss just a month before the French Open.

While clay has never been de Minaur's favourite surface, the 2024 French Open quarterfinalist now has an alarming 4-6 win-loss record in six tournaments since winning the Rotterdam Open in February. He boasted a 13-7 record in the same window last year.

Jodar slammed 15 winners past the world number eight, the last being a glorious inside-out forehand tracer that may have felt like a merciful release for the Aussie, who could only conjure up two winners of his own while making 26 unforced errors.

World number one Jannik Sinner was watching on from courtside as the teenage wildcard showed just why he's shot up from 687th to 42nd in the rankings in just the past 12 months.

"It was crazy. Crazy feelings. I'm super happy to get my second win here in Madrid at my home tournament, where I used to come when I was a kid, and I used to watch all these top players in the stands," Jodar said at the Manolo Santana Stadium at the famed Caja Magica venue.

"Now, being able to play here in the centre court means a lot to me."

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

De Minaur's exit means Adam Walton, who faces 13th seed Karen Khachanov on Saturday, remains the only Australian left in either the men's or women's draw.

Next up, Jodar will play fellow 19-year-old tyro Joao Fonseca in a duel of the two youngest players inside the world's top 100.

With his win over de Minaur, the Spaniard has joined Fonseca as just the second man born in 2006 or later to record a top-10 win.

Jodar will certainly be someone to be avoided in the Roland Garros draw, as he rampages on after capturing his maiden ATP Tour title in Marrakech earlier this month and then making the semifinals of the Barcelona Open.

Earlier, top seed Sinner received an early shock from French qualifier Benjamin Bonzi, losing the first set in a tiebreak before powering to a 6-7 (6/8), 6-1, 6-4 victory.

The ABC of SPORT Sports content to make you think... or allow you not to. A newsletter delivered each Saturday.

AAP

title: When is an apology not an apology?
text:
When is an apology not an apology?

It might start out looking like one.

It might contain all the right words like "regret" and "taking accountability". The person apologising might be owning their share of one of the most consequential moments of our times, and they might even look a fair bit miserable about it.

But, I'm afraid, that doesn't quite cut for me.

I'm rather old school.

You can keep all your fancy words, your saucer-eyed anguish; if I'm not seeing intestines cut from your belly and poured out on the ground before you, or the flesh of your back flagellated, cross-hatched and bleeding, I don't believe it.

Apology, sure — but make it medieval and, dammit, make it work.

Tucker Carlson campaigned powerfully for Trump, mocked and ridiculed his critics, rallied people to his cause on his very powerful platform. (AP: Seth Wenig)

Something has happened recently to the act of publicly saying sorry, and I mean apart from the fact that we are suddenly getting an unexpected rush of them.

We've just heard a major mea culpa from one of Donald Trump’s most impervious champions, the broadcaster Tucker Carlson, and his speechwriting brother, as they accepted blame for the place the world finds itself in because of Trump’s presidency.

In a very different key, a high-profile reality TV contestant has also gone the big sorry about misogynistic stuff he said directly to a camera, while sober, upright, in daylight and with a knowing leer on his face.

But don't worry. He's now really sorry.

We're all wearingly familiar with the generic non-apology: the one forced from the transgressor as a Hail Mary to save a floundering career; or the qualified "sorry IF I hurt you" apology and the like — the meagre crumbs of self-abnegation that ego and id will reluctantly serve up.

But there's almost something weaponised about the contemporary apology, as abject and complete as it seems to be, and perversely the social media culture that aims to teach self-awareness and accountability might just be responsible for it.

'It was not intentional'

Let's look at the language of Carlson's apology and consider what exactly he said he was apologising for.

On his podcast with his brother this week, Carlson said: "It's not enough to say, 'Well, I changed my mind,' or 'Oh, this is bad, I'm out.'

"In very small ways, but in real ways, you and me and millions of people like us are the reason this is happening right now, so I do think it's like a moment to wrestle with our own consciences. You know, we'll be tormented by it for a long time. I will be, and I want to say I'm sorry for misleading people, and it was not intentional."

I've italicised those words myself. I'll come back to them.

Sign up for Weekend Reads in your inbox Start your weekend with the best of the ABC's journalism, presented by Virginia Trioli. Discover compelling features, big ideas and revealing analysis to understand the stories that matter to Australians.

Carlson's relationship with Trump has been bumpy over the decades, but his active public backing has been especially visible and unequivocal from Trump's post-2022 comeback through the 2024 campaign and into 2025–26.

He has intimate knowledge of the man, his character, his choices, his frailties, his tendencies and, like anyone else who had eyes and ears, Tucker would have heard precisely what Trump said he wanted to do if he won office again.

So, I am left wondering: just what is this apology for?

Carlson campaigned powerfully for Trump, mocked and ridiculed his critics, rallied people to his cause on his very powerful platform.

When he says it was not my intention — the grown-up version of the six-year-old mumble, "didn't mean it, mum" — then what exactly did he intend when he decided to throw his lot in with the man who was the central political instigator and beneficiary of the January 6 uprising?

What else could he possibly have intended other than helping a leader and a man like that get elected?

Is he sorry that… he feels bad about it? And wants to make that bad feeling to go away?

Just saying 'sorry' doesn't fix it

Anyone on social media could tell you what's happening here, as the language and approach seems to come straight from the "accountability" culture that popular relationship and psychology accounts have made so familiar.

Hugely popular accounts by people like Esther Perel or Jillian Turecki have made the notion of "owning" accountability for decisions or outcomes a commonly understood and now well-used ploy for signalling self-awareness and growth — but like the quick-form media in which it's delivered, understanding of it seems truncated.

Simply saying that you now get it, that you see the problem, that you're sorry isn't actually accountability.

Why I watch MAFS with my teenage son Never was there a stronger example of “do as I say, not as they do”.

The best example of this is the aforementioned Married At First Sight star, Danny, who played an excellent catfishing game with his partner, made degrading comments about her and other women and then, when caught, bleated that he was "taking accountability" and then wondered why people were still mad.

Here's what Tucker and Danny and anyone else who's been found out needs to understand: just saying sorry doesn't fix it.

Fixing it fixes it.

What that could mean for someone like Carlson is, perhaps, joining the push described by podcaster and former political adviser, Anthony Scaramucci, of some Republican members of Congress to remove Trump from office using constitutional amendment 25.

And for someone like Danny, it might mean maybe just shutting up and going away for a while?

This weekend, like so many other Australians, I'll be remembering the life of broadcaster James Valentine, who I was so fortunate to know as a colleague over many years. He leaves lessons in how to live life well, how to connect with joy and with a sense of community and I will remind myself of the importance of holding that faith.

I was an early adopter of the band he joined in later years. Here is James with The Models, in full flight. Go well, James.

Virginia Trioli is presenter of Creative Types and a former co-host of ABC News Breakfast and Mornings on ABC Radio Melbourne.

title: Family keeps century-old farming trade alive
text:
Tom Hummelstad says he'll put out a blue flag to mark the turn-off.

Without the flag, it's hard to find the place.

It marks the beginning of a short but winding driveway through bush and paddocks sprinkled with black cattle.

This is Gumbaynggirr Country, in the Northern Rivers region of New South Wales.

At the end of the driveway, Tom and his younger brother work away in separate sheds.

Tom says he may be five years older than Paul, but is "five years less wise" by his own admission.

Tom Hummelstad and his brother Paul believe they are the only manufacturers of ear-marking pliers working in Australia today. (ABC Landline: Laurence Curson)

They're plying a trade handed down by their father.

"It was Dad, Harry, he used to make them, and his father Henry, he made them, and then his father Eyvind, he made them," Tom says.

He's talking about ear-marker tools — a small, handheld pair of pliers that allow a farmer to mark and distinguish cattle and sheep in a tradition applied by farmers for more than 100 years.

"It leaves a mark in the ear so you can see daylight through," Paul says.

It is not uncommon for Hummelstad pliers to be sent in for repairs or sharpening that were made by Tom and Paul's great-grandfather. (Supplied)

"It's good because you can walk into a paddock with all your cattle, and the first thing they do is put their ears up and look at you, so you can see the mark in their ear.

"It's sort of like a hole punch or a leather punch, I'd imagine … but the problem with that comparison is that with the ear mark there's over 30,000 different styles."

A trade passed down through generations

Tom reaches for a book, the Authorised "Owners" Sheep Earmarks, dated 1916.

It sets out the many ways in which an ear can be marked and registered with the authorities.

Paul Hummelstad has been making ear-marking pliers for close to 40 years. (ABC Landline)

"Putting what we call a 'cut on' is really important," Tom says, of the trade.

" They've got to come down with a slice, you need to be able to cut newspaper, and once that's done you put them in the fire and harden it. "

The workspace is neat.

There's a tin of International Roast in one corner, freshly filed metal shavings by a vice in another.

Eyvind Ragnvald Hummelstad was Tom and Paul's great-grandfather. (Supplied)

Paul wears a leather apron that looks like it might have been handed down from his forebears, but actually came from eBay for a supposed bargain.

He and Tom believe they are some of the last manufacturers of ear-marking pliers in Australia.

"It's a sad fact, really, just the way fabrication seems to be going in this country. I feel very proud of the fact that we can still do that," Paul says.

With a four-month waiting list on repairs, theirs is a service very much in demand.

Paul tells how Tom recently repaired a pair of pliers, believed to have been made by their grandfather, and also manufactured a new pair for a customer.

" The guy rang up and said: 'Which one is the new pair?' and he was dead serious. "

"That's like driving a T-model Ford around now, and it had only broken down once," Paul says.

Paul Hummelstad and his brother Tom are fourth-generation ear-marker makers. (ABC Landline: Laurence Curson)

The men are in business with their brother, Bill, who makes cattle and horse brands, a trade also handed down.

Customers ranging from cattle owners to nightclubs

The family has made brands for celebrities and media moguls, but Harry Hummelstad reportedly pulled up when asked to make a brand for the Kings Cross Hellfire Club.

Paul recalls how the nightclub put in the order sometime in the late 1980s.

"They rang up and said they wanted a brand with the initials HFC and anyway, Harry said, 'Yes we can do that for you … what is it you'd be branding — cattle or horses?'

"And when they said, 'Humans', my father was in shock and said, 'I can't help you.'"

Tom finishes the story: "But he said he had a mate who would."

Each ear plier is made completely by hand by the Hummelstad brothers much like they were 100 years ago. (Supplied)

Then there was the time Harry accidentally got the J on a brand back to front for one of Kerry Packer's polo ponies.

"My father had a real problem with Js" Paul says.

"I was an apprentice. I'd just started, and I remember the look on Dad's face. He went ghostly white while he was talking on the phone [to the man who bought the brand on Packer's behalf]."

Having hung up in a state of shock, Paul enquired as to what had happened.

" He said, 'I've just made a brand for one of Kerry Packer's polo horses and the J is back to front and they've branded the bastard.' "

Fortunately, as the story goes, Kerry didn't notice, and both Harry and the man who bought the brand agreed there was no need to point it out.

In Australia, it's now mandatory to place an electronic tag in the ear of all sheep and cattle, but the brothers say marking, or notching as it's sometimes known, offers another form of insurance and remains popular with farmers.

Ear-marking pliers are used to mark cattle and sheep so they can be identified. (ABC Landline)

At times, ear marking has been mandatory.

The RSPCA opposes hot iron branding and ear marking.

Paul jokes that his daughter's "doof party" friends have bigger holes in their ears and noses than ear-marked stock.

Tom believes that if the marking is done swiftly and cleanly, the animal will recover quickly.

Some of the tools in the workshop are more than 100 years old.

A photo of the brothers' forefather; Eyvind Ragnvald Hummelstad sits near the desk.

The Norwegian pigeon thief is believed to have had a few issues with the constabulary before he "got himself straight and migrated to Australia in probably the 1880s," according to Tom.

Eyvind married a milliner, who sold enough hats to purchase an ear-marker-making business located in Cunningham St, Haymarket in the middle of Sydney and Hummelstad and Sons was born.

It would continue for at least three more generations, surviving moves to Sydney's western suburbs, Grafton, and now to a pair of sheds at the end of lane marked with a blue flag.

Watch ABC TV's Landline at 12:30pm AEST on Sunday or stream anytime on ABC iview.

title: Anzac biscuit-tin guitars sold to aid veteran charity
text:
If you're looking for Adrian "Ace" Kitson, you'll likely find him in his garage, hunched over a dimly lit workbench, deep in concentration.

A tuning peg in hand, he gently adjusts the strings of his latest creation until it sounds just right.

The Tasmanian musician-turned-guitar-maker has spent the better part of two years handcrafting a collection of cigar-box guitars made from Anzac biscuit tins and local timber.

The guitars are made from biscuit tins by Tasmanian bakery Cripps. (ABC News: Georgia Hogge)

On one, a rifle cartridge functions as a whammy bar. On another, a gun belt is fastened in place of a guitar strap.

Once complete, Mr Kitson intends to sell the guitars and donate the proceeds to Open Arms, a mental health charity that offers military-focused counselling services for veterans.

It is a project close to his heart.

"I've had friends who have served in the armed forces [as well as] family, my grandfather," he said.

" I feel very sorry for a lot of veterans who returned from overseas who suffer terribly from all sorts of mental illnesses. "

The guitars are short — like a banjo — and have fewer strings than a typical guitar. (ABC News: Georgia Hogge)

Mr Kitson knows firsthand the importance of veteran charities such as Open Arms.

Thirty years earlier, an incident on a night out left Mr Kitson with a traumatic brain injury. He says he has had post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) ever since.

Mr Kitson said he was not able to access support when he needed it.

"It kind of spurs me to do what I can to try to help," he said.

Homemade guitars playable

As well as being novel, each of Mr Kitson's creations is a functional instrument, although with a shorter neck and fewer strings than a standard guitar.

When played acoustically, the guitars produce a banjo-like sound due to the tin body.

A retrofitted piezo pick-up means it can be connected to an amplifier.

"You've got to be able to play them," he said.

" What's the point of having them if you can't grab it off the wall and say, 'look what I can do'? "

Despite their unconventional design and short scale, all of the instruments are playable. (ABC News: Georgia Hogge)

While Mr Kitson's goal is ultimately to raise money, he also hopes the guitars can bring joy to those who buy them.

"When you pass them on to someone else and you see the joy in their face when they get it, that's just priceless," he said.

"I love making people happy."

Guitar inspired by VC hero Teddy Sheean

There is one particularly special guitar that will not be for sale.

Honouring local war hero, Edward 'Teddy' Sheean VC, the guitar will be donated to the Latrobe RSL sub-branch to be kept on display.

Sheean, who was born in Lower Barrington in Tasmania's north-west and spent his childhood in Latrobe, held the rank of ordinary sailor in the Royal Australian Navy during World War II.

A statue of Edward 'Teddy' Sheean VC has been erected in Latrobe. (ABC News: Mackenzie Heard)

He was 18 when he died defending his ship from Japanese fire in 1942.

In 2020, he was posthumously awarded a Victoria Cross after a long-running campaign by surviving family members.

"He means a lot to the local area," Jason Bloom, secretary of the Latrobe RSL sub-branch, said.

"We have erected a statue in his honour … the RSL has various mementos of him."

Latrobe RSL secretary Jason Bloom. (ABC News: Mackenzie Heard)

In the four years he has been in his role as secretary, Mr Kitson's guitar is the first physical donation he's received.

"They are awesome. He's done a superb job on them guitars," Mr Bloom said.

"There's a lot of time put into them and hopefully we can sell a few for him."

title: Surfer turns decommissioned wind turbine blades into surfboard fins
text:
At the Waubra Wind Farm near Ballarat, 40-metre turbine blades that once helped power the state are being sent away for a second life.

Far from the frigid, windswept fields of western Victoria, small parts of the fibreglass turbine blades are carving through waves along the New South Wales northern beaches.

Sydney surfer Banjo Hunt has been repurposing a 6m section of a blade from the Waubra farm using a computer-operated cutter to shape fins from the composite.

"I'd tried caravan walls and boat walls, but all of that has a super loose weave fibreglass that doesn't really lend itself to surfboard fins, and so that led me towards wind turbines," Mr Hunt said.

There are questions around how Australia will manage decommissioned wind turbine blades. (ABC News: Charlotte Wilkes)

While 90 per cent of a wind turbine can be recycled, the 10 per cent that cannot is the blades, which is a problem wind farms across the country are racing to solve.

According to the Clean Energy Council, there are 31 Australian wind farms more than 15 years old, and by 2034, decommissioned wind farms are estimated to produce 15,000 tonnes of blade composite waste.

Mr Hunt is offering a potential blueprint for managing the thousands of tonnes of blade waste predicted to hit Australian shores in the next decade.

Time is right

Acciona's Waubra Wind Farm is in its 16th year of operation.

General manager of brand and marketing Caroline Pinter said that while blade recycling was not a pressing issue today, it would inevitably become one.

"Finding solutions for these complex composite materials is not easy, and especially because wind turbine blades are so long," she said.

"The blades at the Waubra Wind Farm are 40 metres long, and modern blades are 80 metres long, so that's a significant amount of material and a significant challenge to transport them."

Mr Hunt said what made these turbine blades so difficult to recycle was also what made the material so perfect for surfboard fins.

Banjo Hunt says it has traditionally been labour-intensive to make fins. (Supplied: Christopher Chan)

He has spent the past few months making fins from a 6m-long portion of a turbine blade.

He said that by reusing the material, it avoided the most expensive and labour-intensive parts of traditional fin manufacturing, as well as keeping his company's fin production in Australia.

"It's quite an abundant resource, and it's certainly a step in the right direction compared to sourcing my materials otherwise, where it's expensive and toxic and comes from places I can't really track down," Mr Hunt said.

Banjo Hunt's surfboard fins are made from 100 per cent turbine blades. (Supplied: Christopher Chan)

According to Acciona, the turbine blade Mr Hunt has used to create fins generated 14,773 megawatt-hours of clean energy, avoiding 19,000 tonnes of carbon dioxide emissions during its operational life.

Tried and tested

Darcy Crump put the surfboard fins to test at the 2026 Australian Boardriders Battle. (Supplied: Christopher Chan)

Professional surfer Darcy Crump put the turbine blade fins to the test at the Australian Boardriders Battle on the Gold Coast last month.

"As a surfer, we're kind of always looking for renewable things, and how we can recycle in [the] best places, and surfboard fins was a pretty big one," Mr Crump said.

"So, having these fins made out of wind turbine blades was pretty special, and it turned a lot of heads while we were up on the coast."

Mr Crump said the fins felt "a little stiffer" than other commercially made ones, but noted that everyone liked something different.

"And for me, they were on par with all the big-name brands out there for fins, so I was happy with how they performed and got to use them at a pretty high level [of competition]."

Darcy Crump says he will keep using the turbine fins for surfing more powerful waves. (Supplied: Christopher Chan)

Solutions beyond the sea

Andrew Bray is the national director of RE-Alliance, an independent not-for-profit organisation helping regional communities in the renewable transition.

He said turning blades into surfboard fins was just one of many possible solutions to a problem that will become increasingly common.

"[Blades] are made up of an epoxy resin and materials which make them really strong and durable during their life, but it also means that they're harder to break down at the end of their life," Mr Bray said.

The Waubra Wind Farm is in regional Victoria. (ABC News: Charlotte Wilkes)

"A lot of recycling and decommissioning is happening in places like Europe already, but it hasn't really started happening here in Australia.

"[Overseas], we're seeing wind turbine blades turned into a different range of things, such as bus shelters, skis, furniture, and playground equipment."

Mr Bray said newer blades were being designed to be more easily recycled, so it was just older blades that required thinking outside the box.

"We're going to need a few [ideas] to make sure that these older blades can be recycled," he said.

title: A fragile truce, and a funeral, launch parties’ scramble for Stafford
text:
Advertisement

Perspective PoliticsQueenslandQueensland government A fragile truce, and a funeral, launch parties’ scramble for Stafford Matt Dennien April 25, 2026 — 1:05pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Save this article for later Add articles to your saved list and come back to them anytime. Got it Share A A A

Agreement among political parties is a rare thing in an election campaign. Queensland, with its winner-takes-all culture, is no exception – even in a single-seat byelection. But, sometimes, it does happen. Hence, the campaign truce struck between Labor and the LNP on Wednesday as mourners farewelled the late Stafford MP Jimmy Sullivan. Whether the truce held is another thing. While there was a notable absence of activity on the ground and on social media, inside the walls of parliament it was a different story. Premier David Crisafulli at Grange petrol station media conference last Friday, within the Stafford electorate, to announce the byelection date and share detail of discussions about a potential new fuel refinery in the state. Matt Dennien Within an hour of MPs filing into the chamber for the later sitting, talk of the May 16 byelection – the snap nature of which caught those outside government off-guard – was in the air.

Advertisement

First out of the gate was Greenslopes Labor MP Joe Kelly, deputy chair of the government-controlled health, environment and innovation committee, with venom levelled at its chair. The LNP’s Rob Molhoek, Kelly said, had agreed to postpone a private meeting of the committee set down for that day and Kelly’s arrangements for a substitute to attend a public one. Mourners entering former Stafford MP Jimmy Sullivan’s funeral at Little Flower Church in Kedron on Wednesday. Felicity Caldwell Instead, an extra public briefing appeared on the schedule with the Metro North Hospital and Health Service for 11.30am – during Sullivan’s funeral, who Kelly said he considered a friend. That meeting was ultimately pushed back to Thursday. But Kelly said to have sprung such a meeting on the committee under such circumstances was “dishonest and disgraceful”.

Advertisement

A few minutes later in question time, Crisafulli was peppered – as he was every day this week – with questions about the electoral enrolment particulars of Sports Minister Tim Mander. Related Article Queensland government ‘He has found the peace that eluded him’: Hundreds farewell MP Jimmy Sullivan Responding to one from deputy opposition leader Cameron Dick about when he was first informed of the relationship between Mander and cabinet colleague Amanda Camm, Crisafulli detoured. After what the premier said was an interjection by Dick during his answer which used the word “Stafford”, Crisafulli launched into his thesis of what the pursuit of Mander was about. “Those who understand politics understand the reality of byelections and what they mean for governments, but in this case we have an opposition that is not going very well at the moment – it is not travelling all that well – and it has a leader who is hanging by a thread,” Crisafulli stated.

Advertisement

“The truth is: keen observers of history will know that the last time there was a byelection in Stafford the opposition secured an 18 per cent swing towards it.” Crisafulli then reiterated the double-digit swing benchmark he has imposed on Opposition Leader Steven Miles lest his Labor leadership come undone. The LNP’s Fiona Hammond, Labor’s Luke Richmond, and Greens’ Jess Lane are among a field of nine contesting the Stafford byelection. LNP, Queensland Labor, Queensland Greens Both Deputy Premier Jarrod Bleijie and Mermaid Beach MP Ray Stevens later also took jabs at Labor’s candidate, and assistant state secretary, Luke Richmond. Richmond, a lawyer with a health policy background, will be joined on the ballot paper by eight others, including the LNP’s 2024 candidate and former Brisbane councillor Fiona Hammond.

Advertisement

Announcing the full suite of nominated candidates after the cut-off and ballot draw on Friday, Electoral Commissioner Pat Vidgen noted such a showing was a strong field for a byelection. Other previously known candidates include Liam Parry, the first person charged under the LNP’s Palestinian protest slogan ban – endorsed by the yet-to-be-registered Queensland Socialists. Jess Lane, who polled 18 per cent for the Greens in 2024, works in education and wears a pro-Palestinian watermelon pin in her campaign material, announced on Friday she would also recontest. These two alone likely help describe the marginal Labor inner-northern suburbs seat’s demographics to those unfamiliar: younger and with higher levels of renters and uni grads than the average.

Advertisement

Advertisement

Even before Lane’s candidacy was announced, Crisafulli was already batting away questions in parliament about any deal for One Nation to run by calling on Labor to reveal any “deal” for appearance on how-to-vote cards of the Greens, which he described as “an antisemitic movement”. Crisafulli and his ministers used the week marking almost 18 months since their return to government to tout successes across crime, health, housing and cost-of-living. Fuel security also remained high on the list of talking points and announcements, as new polling for this masthead showed the LNP losing some skin (even if Crisafulli continues riding high). Labor continued its push for more immediate action on affordability concerns, and has focused much of the campaigning on expansion plans for the local Prince Charles hospital.

Advertisement

Advertisement

Across the 23 for Queensland parliament back to 1996, the average swing against the government has been 4.2 per cent, while the average swing against the incumbent party has been 5.9 per cent. Two of the most recent, for comparison, have produced the biggest shifts in voter support. The high-water swing against a government was 21.5 per cent against Labor in Inala in early 2024, after the resignation of former premier Annastacia Palaszczuk. Meanwhile, the biggest swing to a government came late last year, when the LNP prised Hinchinbrook from the Katter’s Australian Party with a swing of 16.87 per cent. But those keen observers of politics and history invoked by Crisafulli are likely to know exactly those byelection realities for governments of which he spoke about on Wednesday.

title: AFL LIVE: Hawthorn Hawks, Gold Coast Suns open Anzac Day as Ugle-Hagan makes debut
text:
Just two days after handing down a controversial judgment in a case involving a homophobic slur, the AFL has removed its appeals board chairman Will Houghton KC.

The AFL confirmed on Saturday that the decision was effective immediately.

Houghton chaired an appeals board panel on Thursday night that reduced young Saint Lance Collard’s ban for saying the phrase “f---ing f----t” during a VFL match from nine weeks to four weeks (with two games suspended).

League boss Andrew Dillon took the extreme step of condemning the decision on Friday.

He said the AFL disciplinary tribunal’s original ban was “not only warranted - it was necessary”.

Dillon also rejected the board’s assertion that it is commonplace that players use racist, sexist or homophobic language from time to time in “highly competitive” levels of football.

“The AFL strongly rejects the statement not only that such language is commonplace, but also any implication that may be a factor in determining the severity of the sanction,” Dillon said in comments later echoed by the AFL Players Association.

“We will not accept, excuse or normalise behaviour and language that demeans, discriminates or vilifies people based on who they are.”

When asked about the Houghton termination today, a league spokesperson said: “Mr Houghton served as chair since his appointment in March 2024 and has contributed at club level over many years and the AFL thanks him for his service.”
